questions.passage:
The immersive exhibition has acquired the prestige of an artistic form while retaining the instincts of a shopping arcade. Its defenders describe rooms of swelling projections, programmed fragrance, and synchronized sound as a release from the severity of the framed object. Paintings no longer sit at a distance; they engulf us. Yet scale is not the issue. Frescoes, processions, and theatrical scenery have surrounded spectators for centuries. What distinguishes the contemporary immersive show is not that it occupies space, but that it converts the spectator’s attention into a managed resource.

Before a painting, one may linger, retreat, return, or refuse. The work makes demands, but it cannot schedule obedience. An immersive display reverses that relation. Images arrive according to a sequence fixed elsewhere; music announces when awe should rise; darkness removes competing objects; benches face the approved direction. The visitor is praised for “entering” the art precisely when almost every condition of encounter has been predetermined. This is less participation than compliance with unusually flattering décor. Even the visitor’s movement becomes part of a script whose apparent freedom has been carefully priced and timed.

The rhetoric of access conceals the bargain. Because many people feel intimidated by galleries, promoters promise an art without difficulty: no specialized knowledge, no hushed rooms, no fear of misunderstanding. But difficulty is not an aristocratic tax imposed on pleasure. It is often the name for the work’s resistance to our habits. That resistance may be irritating, but irritation can mark the instant when ordinary expectations lose their authority. A portrait that withholds sympathy, a landscape that refuses depth, or a color relation that remains unstable gives the viewer something no choreography can supply: an occasion to revise perception. The immersive show removes that resistance and then congratulates itself for removing the obstacle it calls elitism.

The transformation is clearest when a dead painter becomes a brand whose works are sliced, enlarged, repeated, and made to pulse. Individual decisions disappear into atmosphere. A brushstroke is no longer evidence of pressure, speed, correction, or restraint; it becomes wallpaper with biographical prestige. The artist’s name survives because it helps sell tickets, while the artist’s particular acts are dissolved into a generic promise of intensity. One might as well advertise a symphony and deliver a room whose lights blink in time with three familiar chords.

Advocates reply that such displays lead newcomers toward the original works. Occasionally they may. A souvenir can lead someone toward a book, too, but that possibility does not turn the souvenir into literature. The relevant issue is not whether an experience produces a later benefit. It is whether the experience deserves the artistic authority it claims for itself. An exhibition that openly presents itself as entertainment may be gaudy, ingenious, or delightful. The fraud begins when engineered sensation is treated as a more generous version of attention.

I am not arguing for joyless rooms or for the moral superiority of squinting. Pleasure belongs in art, but there is a difference between pleasure that begins in the work and pleasure that uses the work as a pretext. The former can surprise us because it arises from another intelligence. The latter is calibrated in advance, delivered on cue, and measured by the visitor’s willingness to photograph it. Immersion promises to abolish the distance between spectator and art. In practice, it abolishes the distance between culture and advertising.
questions.stem: Which statement best captures the passage's central argument?

choices.A: Immersive exhibitions replace independent attention with commercially managed sensation.
choices.B: Immersive exhibitions succeed mainly when they reproduce the scale of historical art.
choices.C: Traditional galleries should adopt more theatrical methods to welcome unfamiliar visitors.
choices.D: Original artworks gain cultural value when reproduced through new sensory technologies.

explanations.A: Correct. The author argues that immersive displays predetermine the viewer’s experience and convert artworks into vehicles for engineered sensation and commercial appeal.
explanations.B: The author explicitly denies that sheer scale distinguishes these displays and points to older spatial arts as a contrast.
explanations.C: The author rejects the premise that difficulty should be removed through choreography and does not urge galleries to imitate theatrical entertainment.
explanations.D: The passage claims that enlargement and repetition often erase the particular decisions that give an artwork its force.

questions.stem: The references to frescoes, processions, and theatrical scenery primarily serve to

choices.A: show that spectators have always preferred large works to framed paintings.
choices.B: suggest that contemporary exhibitions faithfully revive older communal art forms.
choices.C: separate spatial magnitude from the managed attention the author criticizes.
choices.D: establish that historical art depended on commercial methods of presentation.

explanations.A: The passage makes no claim that large works have consistently been preferred over framed ones.
explanations.B: The older forms are introduced to deny that contemporary immersion is novel merely because it surrounds the spectator.
explanations.C: Correct. These references show that art has long occupied enveloping spaces, allowing the author to locate the real problem in the programming of attention.
explanations.D: The author does not attribute modern commercial staging to frescoes, processions, or theatrical scenery.

questions.stem: The author's tone toward the promotional language of immersive exhibitions is best described as

choices.B: scornful and protective of viewer autonomy.
choices.C: detached and curious about institutional change.
choices.D: enthusiastic but concerned about artistic standards.

explanations.A: The author does not idealize a lost era with sadness; the language attacks present claims directly.
explanations.B: Correct. Phrases such as “flattering décor” and “the fraud begins” mock the promotional rhetoric while defending the viewer’s freedom to control an encounter.
explanations.C: The passage is openly judgmental rather than detached, and its central distinctions are evaluative.
explanations.D: The author allows that entertainment may be delightful but never expresses enthusiasm for granting it artistic authority.

questions.stem: Which claim is most strongly implied by the discussion of standing before a painting?

choices.A: A static artwork communicates only through prolonged study.
choices.B: A gallery protects art by limiting sensory stimulation.
choices.C: A painting imposes fewer interpretive demands than a projection.
choices.D: A viewer controls the timing of a static encounter.

explanations.A: The author values the possibility of lingering or returning but does not claim that prolonged study is the only route to meaning.
explanations.B: The discussion concerns the viewer’s freedom to approach and withdraw rather than the protective function of a gallery.
explanations.C: The painting is described as resistant and demanding, not as easier to interpret than a projection.
explanations.D: Correct. The ability to linger, retreat, return, or refuse shows that the viewer governs the duration and rhythm of the encounter.

questions.stem: Which finding would most weaken the author's criticism of immersive exhibitions?

choices.A: Visitors freely control viewing sequence and duration while later recalling the artists’ specific formal decisions.
choices.B: Projected displays omit subtle surface variations that remain visible when visitors examine the original paintings.
choices.C: Admission to immersive displays consistently costs more than entry to museums exhibiting the original artworks.
choices.D: Visitors photograph immersive rooms more frequently and share the images more widely on social media.

explanations.A: Correct. This finding would challenge two central criticisms at once: that immersive displays predetermine the encounter and that they erase the artist’s particular decisions. Genuine control over sequence and duration, paired with stronger recall of formal choices, would suggest that immersion can support rather than replace independent attention.
explanations.B: This finding would strengthen the criticism that immersive reproduction dissolves the specific material and formal decisions present in the original work.
explanations.C: Higher prices might support the author’s commercial critique, but price alone does not determine whether attention is managed or artistically serious.
explanations.D: Frequent photography and social sharing would support the author’s claim that the experience is calibrated for spectacle and promotion.

questions.stem: In context, “pleasure that begins in the work” refers to pleasure that

choices.A: depends on prior knowledge of artistic history.
choices.B: emerges from engagement with the artwork’s particular demands.
choices.C: results from removing barriers to immediate comprehension.
choices.D: arises when spectators share a coordinated sensory environment.

explanations.A: The passage never makes historical knowledge a condition of valid pleasure.
explanations.B: Correct. The phrase names a response generated by the artwork’s own resistance, form, and intelligence rather than by an externally programmed spectacle.
explanations.C: The author argues that difficulty can be productive and opposes treating immediate comprehension as the measure of access.
explanations.D: A coordinated environment exemplifies pleasure arranged in advance rather than pleasure originating in the work.
